title: url.parse() is deprecated
message: url.parse() was deprecated in Node.js 11.0. It has known security issues with certain inputs.
suggestion: Use `new URL(urlString)` instead. The URL class is globally available in Node.js 10+ and all browsers.

title: url.resolve() is deprecated
message: url.resolve() was deprecated in Node.js 11.0 alongside url.parse().
suggestion: Use `new URL(resolved, base)` or the WHATWG URL constructor instead.

title: new Buffer() is deprecated
message: new Buffer() is deprecated since Node.js 6.0 due to security and performance concerns. Buffer() with no arguments, a number argument, or a string argument can produce inconsistent behavior.
suggestion: Use `Buffer.from()`, `Buffer.alloc()`, or `Buffer.allocUnsafe()` instead of the new Buffer() constructor.

title: punycode module moved to userland
message: The built-in punycode module was deprecated in Node.js 7.0 and moved to userland. Internal use by Node.js does not guarantee API stability.
suggestion: Install and require the `punycode` package from npm instead, or use `Intl.URLParser` for URL operations.

title: querystring module is deprecated
message: The querystring module was deprecated in Node.js 21.0 in favor of URLSearchParams, which is available globally and handles encoding more correctly.
suggestion: Use the global `URLSearchParams` class instead. It provides the same functionality with better spec compliance.

title: fs.exists() is deprecated
message: fs.exists() is deprecated since Node.js 1.0 due to its callback-based API that does not follow the standard Node.js convention of (err, result).
suggestion: Use `fs.access()` to check file existence, or `fs.stat()` to get file metadata. For checking if a file exists before opening, try opening it and handle the ENOENT error.

title: domain module is deprecated
message: The domain module was deprecated in Node.js 4.0. It is pending removal and not recommended for new code. The try/catch/finally pattern is preferred for error handling.
suggestion: Use async/await with try/catch, or the `async_hooks` module for fine-grained execution context tracking.

title: crypto.createCipher() is deprecated
message: crypto.createCipher() is deprecated since Node.js 10.0. It uses a non-standard key derivation function and is considered insecure.
suggestion: Use `crypto.createCipheriv()` with an explicit initialization vector (IV) instead. The iv parameter provides stronger encryption guarantees.

title: crypto.createDecipher() is deprecated
message: crypto.createDecipher() is deprecated since Node.js 10.0 alongside createCipher. It uses a non-standard key derivation function.
suggestion: Use `crypto.createDecipheriv()` with an explicit initialization vector (IV) instead.

title: process.binding() is an internal API
message: process.binding() is an internal Node.js API that is not subject to semantic versioning guarantees. It can break at any time.
suggestion: Use the public Node.js API instead. If you need native bindings, use `node-gyp` or `node-addon-api`.

title: sys module is deprecated
message: The sys module was removed in Node.js 0.3. Its exports were moved to the util module.
suggestion: Use `require('util')` instead. The util module contains all the utilities previously in sys.

title: _linklist is an internal module
message: The _linklist module is an internal Node.js implementation detail. It is not part of the public API and can change or be removed at any time.
suggestion: Use a standard linked-list implementation from npm, or implement your own using plain JavaScript arrays or objects.

title: Internal _stream_* modules are not public API
message: Internal stream modules prefixed with _stream_ (e.g. _stream_readable, _stream_writable) are not part of Node.js's public API and can change without notice.
suggestion: Use the public `stream` module and its exported classes: `stream.Readable`, `stream.Writable`, `stream.Transform`, etc.

title: util._extend() is deprecated
message: util._extend() is an internal API that was deprecated in Node.js 6.0. It is a copy of Object.assign and is not part of the public API.
suggestion: Use `Object.assign()` or the object spread operator `{...obj1, ...obj2}` instead.

title: util.inherits() is discouraged
message: util.inherits() is discouraged for new code. It is a legacy API that does not support class-based inheritance patterns.
suggestion: Use ES2015 `class` and `extends` keywords for prototypal inheritance instead.

title: util.print() is deprecated
message: util.print() was deprecated in Node.js 0.12 and is not available in newer versions.
suggestion: Use `process.stdout.write()` or `console.log()` instead.

title: util.puts() is deprecated
message: util.puts() was deprecated in Node.js 0.12. It is a legacy utility for writing to stdout.
suggestion: Use `console.log()` or `process.stdout.write()` instead.

title: Use async request handling with error boundaries
message: While not strictly deprecated, using raw callback-based http.createServer() without proper error handling is error-prone. Modern Node.js patterns use frameworks with middleware or async error handling.
suggestion: Consider using Express, Fastify, or the built-in async request listener pattern with proper try/catch wrapping.

title: fs.truncateSync() usage requires care
message: fs.truncateSync() is valid but truncating files synchronously in Node.js event loop is discouraged in production code. The file descriptor variant fs.ftruncateSync is sometimes preferred.
suggestion: Prefer the async `fs.truncate()` in production code to avoid blocking the event loop.

title: vm module has security caveats
message: The vm module is not deprecated, but `vm.runInThisContext()` and `vm.createContext()` do not provide security isolation — sandboxed code can escape the sandbox. AI-generated code often misuses it as a security boundary.
suggestion: Do not use the vm module for security isolation. Use real process-level isolation (worker_threads, child_process) or a dedicated sandbox library.

title: require.resolve() usage in application code
message: require.resolve() is primarily intended for tooling and bundler internals. Using it in application code can lead to brittle module resolution paths.
suggestion: Use `import.meta.resolve` (ESM) or `createRequire(import.meta.url).resolve` for explicit path resolution.

title: execSync() usage is discouraged
message: child_process.execSync() blocks the Node.js event loop entirely, which can cause severe performance issues in production servers.
suggestion: Use the async `child_process.exec()` with proper error handling, or use `child_process.spawn()` for streaming output.

title: require.extensions is deprecated
message: require.extensions is a legacy API that was used to modify how Node.js resolves file extensions. It is deprecated because it modifies global state and leads to brittle behavior.
suggestion: Use ESM loader hooks or a custom transpiler pipeline if you need custom file extension handling.

title: dgram socket.ref()/unref() misuse is common
message: While not deprecated, dgram socket.ref() and socket.unref() usage in AI-generated code often indicates misunderstanding of the event loop lifetime. Overusing unref() can cause the process to exit prematurely.
suggestion: Understand the event loop impact before using ref()/unref(). Only unref() sockets when you intentionally want the process to exit without waiting for the socket.
